questions.question: What does the CSS property 'aspect-ratio: 16 / 9' do?
options: Sets the element's width to 16px and height to 9px
Maintains a 16:9 ratio between width and height
questions.explanation: aspect-ratio defines the proportional relationship between width and height. 16/9 means the width will be 16 units for every 9 units of height.

questions.question: What happens if you set both 'width' and 'height' explicitly along with 'aspect-ratio'?
options: The explicit width and height win, aspect-ratio is ignored
Only the smaller explicit dimension wins
questions.explanation: When both width and height are explicitly set, aspect-ratio is ignored. The property only calculates one dimension from the other.

questions.question: Which CSS property should you combine with aspect-ratio to control how an image fills its box?
questions.explanation: object-fit controls how an image or video fills its content box. cover fills and crops, contain shows all content with letterboxing.

questions.question: What does 'aspect-ratio: 1' mean?
questions.explanation: aspect-ratio: 1 is shorthand for 1/1, creating a perfect square where width equals height.

questions.question: What was the common technique for creating aspect ratios before the aspect-ratio property?
questions.explanation: The padding-bottom hack used percentage-based padding (e.g., padding-bottom: 56.25% for 16:9) on a wrapper element with position: absolute on the inner element.

questions.question: What does 'aspect-ratio: auto' do on an element?
options: Uses the image's intrinsic aspect ratio
questions.explanation: auto tells the browser to use the element's intrinsic aspect ratio — the natural ratio of the image file.

questions.question: What does the two-value syntax 'aspect-ratio: auto 16 / 9' do?
options: Creates a 16:9 box with auto margins
Uses intrinsic ratio if available, otherwise 16:9
Sets the ratio to auto on mobile and 16:9 on desktop
questions.explanation: The two-value syntax provides a fallback: auto uses the element's intrinsic ratio, and 16/9 is used if no intrinsic ratio exists.

questions.question: What is the padding-bottom percentage for a 4:3 aspect ratio?
questions.explanation: For 4:3 ratio: 3/4 × 100 = 75%. Padding-bottom is calculated as (height / width) × 100%.

questions.question: Which browser versions support aspect-ratio?
questions.explanation: CSS aspect-ratio is supported in Chrome 89+, Firefox 87+, Safari 15+, and Edge 89+. IE does not support it.

questions.question: Why is aspect-ratio better than the padding-bottom hack?
options: It's faster to write and doesn't need wrapper elements
It works with all HTML elements
questions.explanation: aspect-ratio is cleaner (no wrapper needed), more intuitive (16/9 vs 56.25%), and doesn't require position: absolute on the inner element.

questions.question: What should you do if an element with aspect-ratio contains text that might overflow?
options: Set overflow: auto or add min-height
questions.explanation: Text-heavy elements with aspect-ratio may clip content. Set overflow: auto to allow scrolling or add a min-height safety net.

questions.question: What happens to aspect-ratio when an element has width: auto in a flex container?
options: It works the same as in block layout
The flex container's cross-axis alignment affects the effective ratio
Aspect-ratio is ignored in flex contexts
questions.explanation: In flex containers, items stretch by default (align-items: stretch), which can affect aspect-ratio calculations. Adjust alignment as needed.

questions.question: What is the aspect-ratio for standard widescreen video?
questions.explanation: Standard widescreen video uses a 16:9 aspect ratio. This is the most common ratio for modern displays and video content.

questions.question: If you set 'width: 100%' and 'aspect-ratio: 16/9' on a div in a 400px-wide container, what will the height be?
Which object-fit value should you use to ensure an image fills its aspect-ratio box without distortion, cropping content?
questions.explanation: object-fit: contain shows the entire image within the box while preserving aspect ratio, adding letterboxing if needed. cover crops but fills completely.

questions.question: Does aspect-ratio work on replaced elements like <canvas> and <video>?
options: Yes, it works on all elements
questions.explanation: aspect-ratio works on all HTML elements — replaced (img, video, canvas) and non-replaced (div, section, p).

questions.question: What CSS at-rule can detect aspect-ratio support for fallbacks?
questions.explanation: @supports not (aspect-ratio: 16 / 9) can detect missing support and provide fallback styles for older browsers.

questions.question: What is the decimal equivalent of a 4:3 aspect ratio?
questions.explanation: 4/3 = 1.333... The decimal represents how many times wider the element is compared to its height.

questions.question: What happens to aspect-ratio if neither width nor height is constrained?
options: The element takes the ratio from the parent
The element collapses to zero dimensions
The browser defaults to a 1:1 ratio
questions.explanation: If neither dimension is constrained (e.g., a block element with width: auto and no container width), aspect-ratio has no visible effect.

questions.question: What is the most common use case for 'aspect-ratio: auto 16/9'?
options: Images that might not load — use intrinsic ratio if loaded, 16:9 if not
Videos that should always be 16:9 regardless of source
Responsive containers that switch ratio at breakpoints
Elements that should be square on mobile and 16:9 on desktop
questions.explanation: auto 16/9 on an img means: if the image has loaded (has intrinsic ratio), use that; otherwise use 16:9 as a placeholder ratio.
